ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic spring roll entry; no specific fried pork spring roll entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers spring rolls generically; no pork-specific fried spring roll sub-code identified. Approximate match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fried pork spring roll; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 pieces of fried pork roll, approximately 120 g total, as served at the restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, roasted / BBQ pork roll (spring roll type) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. The HPB Foodo database entry for fried pork spring roll / pork roll is used as the primary nutrient anchor. Carbohydrate is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source record; this is noted as a likely underestimate given the spring roll wrapper (see notes). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork, ground, cooked (NDB 10219) as a supplementary source for fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific fried pork roll. The HPB source reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g, which would render GI not applicable; however, the spring roll wrapper is a wheat-flour-based product that contributes refined carbohydrate not fully captured in the HPB record. GI is estimated at 50 (low) by analogy with fried spring rolls / egg rolls (GI ~50–55) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100; because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g the calculated GL is 0.0, but this is likely an underestimate. Actual GL may be moderate once wrapper carbohydrate is accounted for.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; potassium is very low at 47 mg/100g, providing no offsetting benefit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this item or consume only a very small portion infrequently; the sodium content of 1050 mg/100g is critically high and the sodium-to-potassium ratio is strongly unfavourable for blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g but the spring roll wrapper likely contributes refined wheat-flour carbohydrate not captured in the source data; deep-frying adds oxidised fats that may worsen insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this item with caution: the high protein content (19.1 g/100g) is favourable, but the likely undercounted wrapper carbohydrate and deep-fried fat profile may impair glycaemic control and insulin sensitivity with regular consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g, fat at 10.5 g/100g from deep-frying, and cholesterol at 89 mg/100g collectively present a high cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this item due to critically high sodium, deep-fried fat content, and elevated cholesterol, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; pork contributes arachidonic acid; high sodium may worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried pork preparations; grilled or baked pork with reduced sodium seasoning is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Signature Fried Pork Roll (招牌烤肉炸春卷) is a Singaporean Chinese deep-fried appetiser or snack consisting of seasoned minced or sliced pork and mixed vegetables encased in a thin wheat-flour spring roll wrapper and deep-fried until golden and crunchy. It is served in portions of two pieces with an in-house dipping sauce. The exterior is crisp and blistered from deep-frying, while the interior is savoury, moist, and flavourful from the pork marinade, which typically includes soy sauce, garlic, and a proprietary seasoning blend. Nutritionally, the HPB-sourced data reports 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, and 10.5 g fat per 100 g, with sodium at a critically high 1050 mg/100g. Carbohydrate is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the source, but this is likely an underestimate because the spring roll wrapper contributes refined wheat-flour carbohydrate. Clinically, the item is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and fat profile, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the likely undercounted wrapper carbohydrate and deep-fried fat. It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to AGE formation from deep-frying. The dish is commonly found in Singaporean Chinese restaurants and hawker-adjacent eateries, served as a starter or side dish at lunch and dinner.

confidence_reason: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Core macros are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Saturated fat is supplemented from USDA SR Legacy for pork. The zero carbohydrate value is flagged as a likely source limitation rather than a true zero.
notes: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g per the HPB source record; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, but is likely an underestimate for this dish because the spring roll wrapper is made from refined wheat flour and would contribute carbohydrate in practice — verify with vendor or re-analyse. sugar_g is 0.0 g/100g per HPB source — confirmed zero per HPB. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100g per HPB source — confirmed zero per HPB; likely underestimated given vegetable filling. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this specific fried pork roll preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific dish are not reported in the HPB source record and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact preparation as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat of 3.5 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork ground cooked (NDB 10219) as a supplementary source; this is an approximation. GI is estimated at 50 by analogy with fried spring rolls per University of Sydney GI Database; actual GI may differ once wrapper carbohydrate is properly quantified. Halal status is not confirmed; pork is a primary ingredient and this item is not halal.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input. Identified carbohydrate_g = 0.0 requires zero-macro justification — added to notes. Added saturated_fat_g estimated from USDA SR Legacy. Added all mandatory multilingual local names for Singaporean dish. Added GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database. Verified aliases_by_language strings all present in flat aliases array.

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback. Corrected `foodon_match_type` and `foodex2_match_type` from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values. Corrected `gi_category` from 'medium' to 'low' for a GI of 50, as per standard GI classification (GI <= 55 is low). Self-scored again.
